topic: A student is accused of plagiarism
raw_output:
[story 1]

The accusation landed in the lecture hall like a physical blow. Sarah felt the blood drain from her face, leaving a cold, clammy sensation spreading through her chest. She stared at the accusing finger of the professor, the fluorescent lights above seeming to hum with malicious intent. Every word spoken in the ensuing interrogation felt sharp, dissecting her carefully constructed world. She kept her hands clenched so tightly in her lap that her knuckles turned white, a silent, involuntary tremor running through her arms. *This isn't fair,* she thought, the realization a bitter taste in her mouth. She wanted to shrink into the cheap plastic chair, to disappear into the worn upholstery.

[story 2]

"I can't believe this," Mark muttered, pushing the printed accusation across the desk with a jerky movement. He looked at the highlighted passages, the familiar phrasing of his own thoughts rendered as damning evidence. A wave of nausea washed over him, making the room tilt slightly. He rubbed his temples, the pressure behind his eyes building into a throbbing ache. When the committee began their questioning, Mark simply stared at the floor tiles, his gaze fixed on a single scuff mark, refusing to meet anyone's eyes. His voice, when he finally spoke, was thin and reedy, betraying a profound sense of being utterly exposed and terribly wrong.

[story 3]

The air in the office was thick and stagnant, smelling faintly of old paper and stale coffee. When the dean laid the evidence on the mahogany desk, Amelia felt a sudden, profound emptiness settle in her stomach. Her breath hitched, shallow and rapid. She didn't look up immediately; instead, she focused intently on the grain of the wood, tracing its imperfections with her eyes. When she finally lifted her gaze, her expression was one of utter deflation, a stark lack of energy. "I... I don't understand," she managed, the words barely a whisper, laced with a tremor that seemed to originate deep within her core. She felt utterly hollowed out, as if the very substance of her work had been scooped out and replaced with ash.
